description: Driving for Life and Privately Owned Vehicle (POV) safety training. Covers distracted driving, DUI/DWI, seatbelt use, motorcycle safety, fatigue driving, road rage, winter driving, vehicle maintenance, accident reporting, and the TRiPS system. Based on DoDI 6055.04.

questions.q: What is the leading cause of death for military members off-duty?
questions.a: Privately Owned Vehicle (POV) accidents are the leading cause of off-duty fatalities among military members. POV mishaps consistently account for more military deaths than combat in most years, making traffic safety a critical readiness issue.

questions.q: What is DoDI 6055.04?
questions.a: DoDI 6055.04, DoD Traffic Safety Program, establishes policy and assigns responsibilities for traffic safety on DoD installations and for DoD personnel operating motor vehicles. It covers POV and government vehicle safety, motorcycle requirements, seatbelt use, and impaired driving policies.

questions.q: What is the TRiPS system?
questions.a: The Travel Risk Planning System (TRiPS) is an online risk assessment tool that helps military personnel evaluate risks before taking a trip. It considers factors like distance, fatigue, weather, road conditions, and driving experience. Many commands require TRiPS assessments before holiday travel or long-distance drives.

questions.q: What is the '2-second rule' for following distance?
questions.a: The 2-second rule states you should maintain at least a 2-second following distance behind the vehicle ahead under normal conditions. In adverse conditions (rain, fog, night), increase to 4 seconds or more. At highway speeds, 2 seconds equals roughly one car length per 10 mph.

questions.q: What are the effects of alcohol on driving ability?
questions.a: Alcohol impairs judgment, reaction time, vision, coordination, and concentration. At 0.08 BAC (the legal limit in all states), drivers have significantly reduced ability to control speed and process information. Impairment begins at the first drink, well before the legal limit is reached.

questions.q: What is the legal BAC limit for driving?
questions.a: The legal Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC) limit is 0.08% in all 50 states for drivers 21 and older. For commercial drivers, the limit is 0.04%. For drivers under 21, most states have zero-tolerance laws (0.00-0.02%). Military members may face administrative action at lower BAC levels.

questions.q: What military consequences can result from a DUI/DWI?
questions.a: Military consequences include: UCMJ action (Article 111 — Drunken or Reckless Driving), non-judicial punishment (Article 15/NJP), court-martial, loss of driving privileges on installations, negative evaluation/fitness report, administrative separation, loss of security clearance, reduction in rank, and career damage.

questions.q: What is distracted driving?
questions.a: Distracted driving is any activity that diverts attention from driving. The three types are: Visual (eyes off the road), Manual (hands off the wheel), and Cognitive (mind off driving). Texting is the most dangerous because it combines all three. Cell phone use while driving is prohibited on all DoD installations.

questions.q: What is the DoD policy on cell phone use while driving?
questions.a: DoD prohibits the use of hand-held cell phones while driving on all DoD installations. This includes talking, texting, emailing, or any other use that requires holding the device. Hands-free devices are permitted but discouraged because cognitive distraction remains. Violations can result in loss of installation driving privileges.

questions.q: What are the signs of fatigued driving?
questions.a: Signs include: frequent yawning, heavy eyelids, drifting between lanes, missing exits or turns, difficulty remembering the last few miles driven, hitting rumble strips, following too closely, and difficulty maintaining speed. Fatigue impairs driving ability similarly to alcohol — 18 hours awake equals approximately 0.05 BAC.

questions.q: What should you do if you become drowsy while driving?
questions.a: Pull over to a safe location and take a 15-20 minute nap. Other strategies: switch drivers, get fresh air, consume caffeine (takes 30 minutes to take effect). DO NOT rely on rolling down windows, turning up the radio, or slapping yourself — these do not effectively combat drowsiness.

questions.q: What motorcycle training is required for military personnel?
questions.a: All military personnel who ride motorcycles must complete an approved Motorcycle Safety Foundation (MSF) Basic Rider Course before operating on or off an installation. Experienced riders must complete the MSF Experienced Rider Course. Annual refresher training or advanced courses may be required by the Service.

questions.q: What PPE is required for motorcycle riders on DoD installations?
questions.a: Required PPE includes: DOT-approved helmet, impact-resistant eye protection, long-sleeved shirt or jacket, long trousers, full-fingered gloves, and sturdy over-the-ankle footwear. High-visibility upper garment (vest, jacket, or reflective material) is required during hours of darkness.

questions.q: What are the ATGATT principles for motorcycle safety?
questions.a: ATGATT means 'All The Gear, All The Time.' It emphasizes wearing full protective equipment on every ride regardless of distance, temperature, or speed. This includes helmet, jacket, gloves, boots, and eye protection. Most motorcycle fatalities involve riders without proper gear.

questions.q: What is the seatbelt policy for military personnel?
questions.a: All military personnel must wear seatbelts at all times when operating or riding in a motor vehicle, both on and off DoD installations. This applies to POVs, government vehicles, and rental cars. Failure to wear seatbelts can result in military discipline and loss of installation driving privileges.

questions.q: What is road rage and how should you handle it?
questions.a: Road rage is aggressive or violent behavior stemming from driving. To handle it: avoid eye contact with aggressive drivers, do not make gestures, do not tailgate, give aggressive drivers space, do not engage or retaliate, and call law enforcement if threatened. If followed, drive to a well-lit public area or police station.

questions.q: What are the key winter driving safety tips?
questions.a: Clear all snow/ice from the vehicle before driving, reduce speed, increase following distance (8-10 seconds), brake gently to avoid skidding, never use cruise control on icy roads, carry an emergency kit (blanket, flashlight, water, food, sand/kitty litter), and ensure tires have adequate tread and inflation.

questions.q: What should an emergency car kit contain?
questions.a: A basic kit should include: flashlight with batteries, first aid kit, jumper cables, blankets, water, non-perishable snacks, warning triangles/flares, ice scraper, tire gauge, basic tools, phone charger, and seasonal items (sunscreen in summer, extra warm clothing in winter).

questions.q: What should you do if your vehicle begins to hydroplane?
questions.a: Ease off the gas pedal gradually, do NOT brake suddenly, keep the steering wheel straight or gently steer in the direction you want to go. Wait for the tires to regain traction. Hydroplaning occurs when tires lose contact with the road surface due to water — it can happen at speeds as low as 35 mph.

questions.q: What is the proper technique for driving in fog?
questions.a: Use low-beam headlights (high beams reflect off fog and reduce visibility), reduce speed significantly, increase following distance, use fog lights if equipped, avoid passing other vehicles, use the right edge of the road or lane markings as a guide, and pull over if visibility becomes too poor to drive safely.

questions.q: What is defensive driving?
questions.a: Defensive driving is operating a vehicle in a manner that utilizes safe driving strategies to identify hazards and make well-informed decisions. Key principles: stay alert, expect the unexpected, maintain safe following distance, control speed, scan intersections, check mirrors frequently, and always have an escape route.

questions.q: What is the SIPDE process for safe driving?
questions.a: SIPDE stands for: Search (scan ahead and to the sides), Identify (recognize potential hazards), Predict (anticipate what might happen), Decide (determine your action), and Execute (carry out your decision). It is a systematic approach to hazard awareness and risk reduction while driving.

questions.q: What is the most dangerous time to drive?
questions.a: Nighttime driving is the most dangerous — roughly half of all traffic deaths occur at night despite less traffic. Friday and Saturday nights are the most dangerous due to impaired drivers. Dawn and dusk are also high-risk times due to sun glare and reduced visibility during transition lighting.

questions.q: What are the leading factors in POV fatalities among military members?
questions.a: The top factors are: excessive speed, alcohol/drug impairment, not wearing seatbelts, fatigue/drowsiness, and distracted driving. Weekend and holiday periods see the highest fatality rates. The 18-25 age group is at the highest risk. Single-vehicle accidents (run off road, rollover) are the most common type.

questions.q: What vehicle maintenance checks should be performed regularly?
questions.a: Regular checks include: tire pressure and tread depth, engine oil level, coolant level, brake fluid, windshield washer fluid, battery terminals, headlights/taillights/turn signals, windshield wipers, belts and hoses, and air filter. Follow the manufacturer's maintenance schedule for oil changes, tire rotation, and major services.

questions.q: What is the minimum tire tread depth for safe driving?
questions.a: The minimum legal tread depth is 2/32 of an inch in most states. The 'penny test' can check this: insert a penny upside down into the tread groove — if you can see the top of Lincoln's head, the tread is too worn and the tire needs replacement. Replace tires at 4/32 for wet weather safety.

questions.q: What should you do after a vehicle accident?
questions.a: Stop immediately, check for injuries and call 911, move to a safe location if possible, exchange information with other drivers (name, insurance, license plate), document the scene with photos, do not admit fault, report to your chain of command, and notify your insurance company. On installation, also notify the military police.

questions.q: What is the 'Fatal Vision' concept used in safety training?
questions.a: Fatal Vision goggles simulate the visual impairment caused by alcohol intoxication. Wearing them while performing tasks (walking a line, catching a ball) demonstrates how alcohol affects coordination, depth perception, and reaction time. They are a common tool in military DUI prevention training.

questions.q: Why is speeding dangerous beyond just the risk of a ticket?
questions.a: Speeding reduces the driver's ability to steer safely around curves or obstacles, extends stopping distance, increases the severity of crashes (crash energy increases exponentially with speed), reduces the effectiveness of seatbelts and airbags, and increases the risk of loss of vehicle control.

questions.q: What is the safe following distance for a motorcycle?
questions.a: Motorcyclists should maintain at least a 4-second following distance — double the standard 2-second rule for cars. Motorcycles require greater following distance because they have less crash protection, are less visible, and need more time and space to execute evasive maneuvers.

questions.q: What are the requirements for child restraint systems?
questions.a: Children under 2 must be in a rear-facing car seat. Children 2-4 should be in a forward-facing seat with harness. Children 4-8 should use a booster seat. Children 12 and under should ride in the back seat. All child restraint systems must meet FMVSS 213 standards. Check state law as specific requirements vary.

questions.q: What is the role of the unit safety officer/NCO in POV safety?
questions.a: The unit safety officer/NCO conducts pre-holiday safety briefs, ensures TRiPS assessments are completed, investigates vehicle mishaps, tracks safety trends, conducts motorcycle mentorship programs, facilitates defensive driving courses, and ensures compliance with installation driving policies.

questions.q: What is the significance of the '100 Deadliest Days of Summer'?
questions.a: The period from Memorial Day to Labor Day is called the '100 Deadliest Days' because traffic fatalities spike during summer months. Contributing factors include increased travel, more young drivers, motorcycle riding, fatigue from outdoor activities, and higher rates of impaired driving at summer events.

questions.q: What should you do if a tire blows out while driving?
questions.a: Grip the steering wheel firmly, do NOT slam on the brakes, gradually ease off the accelerator, steer straight or gently in the direction you want to go, activate hazard lights, and slowly move to the shoulder. The natural deceleration from the blown tire will slow the vehicle. Braking hard can cause loss of control.

questions.q: What is the proper technique for handling a skid on ice?
questions.a: Ease off the accelerator (do NOT brake), steer gently in the direction you want the front of the vehicle to go (turn into the skid), and avoid sudden inputs. Once traction is regained, straighten the wheel. For vehicles with ABS, apply firm steady brake pressure — the ABS will prevent wheel lockup.

questions.q: What are the dangers of tailgating?
questions.a: Tailgating eliminates reaction time and stopping distance, is a leading cause of rear-end collisions, increases road rage incidents, and prevents the driver from seeing road hazards ahead of the lead vehicle. At 60 mph, a vehicle travels 88 feet per second — even a 1-second following distance provides almost no margin for error.

questions.q: What visibility precautions should motorcyclists take?
questions.a: Wear high-visibility clothing and reflective gear, use headlights at all times (even daytime), position yourself in the lane where you are most visible to other drivers, avoid riding in blind spots, use hand signals in addition to turn signals, and make eye contact with drivers at intersections before proceeding.

questions.q: What is the relationship between speed and stopping distance?
questions.a: Stopping distance increases exponentially with speed. At 30 mph, total stopping distance (perception + reaction + braking) is approximately 109 feet. At 60 mph, it increases to approximately 304 feet — nearly triple. Doubling speed quadruples the braking distance because kinetic energy increases with the square of velocity.

questions.q: What should be included in a pre-trip vehicle inspection?
questions.a: Check tires (pressure, tread, damage), lights (headlights, brake lights, turn signals), fluids (oil, coolant, washer fluid), wipers, mirrors, horn, brakes, fuel level, and ensure the windshield is clean. For long trips, also check spare tire, jack, and emergency kit. Address any issues before departing.

questions.q: What is the 'Smith System' of defensive driving?
questions.a: The Smith System consists of five keys: 1) Aim High in Steering (look ahead 15 seconds), 2) Get the Big Picture (scan the full environment), 3) Keep Your Eyes Moving (avoid fixating on one point), 4) Leave Yourself an Out (maintain an escape route), 5) Make Sure They See You (use signals, lights, eye contact). It is widely used in military driving courses.
